headline: Notre Dame's ground game breaks through in dominant Week 7 statement win
body: Notre Dame's offensive line imposed its will early and often, establishing a ground presence that Michigan's defense simply could not contain. While the final rushing yardage totals don't scream dominance at 163 yards, the efficiency and timing of those carries set up play-action opportunities that left Michigan's secondary in constant flux. CJ Carr completed 26 of 42 passes for 349 yards and three touchdowns, but the true story was how Notre Dame's staff engineered an offense that kept Michigan off balance. The Irish moved the ball with a methodical confidence that suggested they understood exactly what the Wolverines were trying to do.

Michigan's passing attack, led by QB Browne's 255 yards and two scores, showed flashes of competence, particularly through the arm of WR Wendtland, who hauled in 76 yards on just two receptions. Yet the turnovers proved catastrophic. Two fumbles lost when Notre Dame surrendered none told the entire subplot. Michigan's run game evaporated for just three yards—a number that speaks to how thoroughly the Irish front seven controlled the line of scrimmage. Brock managed only four carries, a clear indicator that Michigan's offensive line never found traction against a Notre Dame defense that smelled opportunity and capitalized.

The 16-point margin obscures how thoroughly Notre Dame controlled field position and tempo. This wasn't a shootout or a back-and-forth affair. It was methodical, disciplined football from the staff that refused to beat itself while systematically beating Michigan. In a season where margins matter, Notre Dame demonstrated the kind of complementary football that separates contenders from pretenders.
turningPoint: Notre Dame's defense forced a fumble late in the second quarter with the game still within reach for Michigan, and the Irish scored immediately after to establish a two-touchdown lead they never relinquished.

unsungHero.name: DEF Manning
unsungHero.why: Four tackles and a sack don't jump off the stat sheet in a 35-19 game, but Manning's disruptive presence in Michigan's backfield set the tone for Notre Dame's defensive consistency when it mattered most.
